variables.content: When fitting the finite-\\(N\\) effective-mass data \\(m^{*}(N)\\) to the scaling form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\), the proposition is: performing repeated fits with Monte Carlo resampling of the statistical error bars on the input finite-\\(N\\) \\(m^{*}(N)\\) values yields uncertainty estimates for the fitted thermodynamic-limit parameter \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) that accurately capture the dominant contribution of the statistical variability in the data; these Monte Carlo-derived error bars are sufficiently conservative to represent the quoted statistical uncertainties in the extrapolated \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), while acknowledging that they may not fully account for model-selection or other systematic sources of error.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

Given Monte Carlo ensembles, reweighting procedures, Newton–Raphson zero-finding, and finite-size fits used to determine $\kappa_0^c(\lambda)$ and ultimately $\lambda_c$, the proposition is that the authors' combination of jackknife resampling for statistical errors and quoted propagation of fit uncertainties yields error bars on $\kappa_0^c$ and on the linearly extrapolated $\lambda_c=0.00116(16)$ that correctly reflect both statistical fluctuations and the dominant systematic uncertainties (including reweighting overlap errors and finite-size fit-model choices).

The DMC effective-mass estimator implemented in these calculations accumulates per-diagram measurements of the inverse effective mass (i.e. estimates of 1/m_{*}(\alpha)) and reports m_{*}^{MC} by inverting the sample mean of these inverse-mass measurements; as the coupling \alpha increases and the true effective mass m_{*}(\alpha) grows rapidly, the sampled inverse-mass values become very small with relative fluctuations comparable to the mean, which leads to large relative statistical uncertainty after inversion and thus to degraded accuracy and stability of the reported m_{*} estimates, an effect that is particularly severe in two dimensions for \alpha>2.

For the Monte Carlo estimates of F(κ,f;w_0,f_0) and Λ(κ,f;w_0,f_0) obtained by parallel tempering and Bridge sampling, the authors applied bootstrap resampling to compute error bars; the proposition is that the implemented bootstrap procedure (resampling Monte Carlo draws and/or replicated runs as done in the experiments) produces uncertainty estimates that adequately reflect the principal sources of estimation error—finite Monte Carlo sampling, Bridge-sampling variability, and finite-data realization variability—for the plotted error bars and reported standard deviations in the numerical results.

Denote by $\sigma_{\mathrm{MC}}$ the statistical standard error from Monte Carlo sampling in a fixed‑phase DMC run for per‑particle energies and by $\sigma_{\mathrm{extrap}}$ the uncertainty arising from the finite‑size correction and extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit; for the DMC calculations carried out (system sizes and sampling lengths used), $\sigma_{\mathrm{MC}}\ll\sigma_{\mathrm{extrap}}$, so that the quoted error bars and the uncertainty budget are dominated by extrapolation systematics rather than by Monte Carlo statistical noise.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For a homogeneous two-dimensional system of identical bosons of mass $m$ interacting via a short-range potential characterized by a microscopic length scale $r_{0}$ and two-dimensional $s$-wave scattering length $a$, DMC ground-state energy per particle $E(N)/N$ was computed for finite particle numbers $N$ in the range $100\le N\le 800$ and these finite-$N$ results were extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit $N\to\infty$ by fitting the computed $E(N)/N$ versus $1/N$ (or another smooth finite-size ansatz) so that the systematic finite-size error of the extrapolated $E/N$ values is smaller than the reported stochastic (statistical) error bars for densities down to gas parameter $n a^{2}=10^{-100}$, where $n$ is the number density and the quoted statistical error bars are the Monte Carlo uncertainties reported for each extrapolated $E/N$.

Consider the reported DMC calculations carried out with finite DMC time steps (time steps of 0.04, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for paramagnetic HEGs and 0.01, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for ferromagnetic HEGs) and using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that, according to extrapolation of variational Monte Carlo energies to zero energy variance, retrieve more than 99% of the correlation energy. The proposition is: the residual biases arising from the fixed-node approximation and from finite DMC time-step errors (noting that halving the time step was verified to have a negligible effect on the computed energy band) are small compared with the overall uncertainties introduced by the finite-size extrapolation procedure; therefore these residual numerical biases do not materially shift the reported extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\).
